nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons, reef flats, passages, and outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 0-32 m, usually amongst coarse rubble or rock.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-West Pacific coral reefs and reef-associated rubble zones, where adults are typically seen solitarily or in small groups.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on pelagic materials such as copepods, isopods, and pelagic tunicates, plus opportunistic algae grazing. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of quality marine flakes and pellets, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafoods, and algae-based foods such as spirulina. Feed once or twice daily, offering only what can be consumed in a few minutes.

avoidWith: newly added tankmates after the damsel is established
delicate sessile invertebrates if reef compatibility is a concern

summary: Hardy Indo-West Pacific damselfish that begins life as a vivid orange-and-blue juvenile (sometimes traded as 'Fire Damsel') and matures into a more subdued brown adult. Tends to become territorial with age, so generally kept singly in modest reef setups.
careNotes: Generally considered one of the easier marine fish for beginners — tolerates a fairly wide range of conditions and accepts prepared foods readily. Juveniles show a striking orange dorsal half with bright blue horizontal lines on a brown body (the look that earned it the 'Fire Damsel' trade name), darkening to a mostly brown adult coloration with a white caudal fin. Has been reported to become noticeably more territorial as it matures, particularly toward conspecifics and other damselfishes; tends to do best as a single specimen or as a same-time-introduced bonded pair. Provide plenty of live rock with caves, crevices, and rubble for shelter, plus open swimming space in the mid water column. Reef compatibility is mixed across sources: LiveAquaria and Reeflings list it as reef-safe with monitoring, while Quality Marine flags occasional picking at benthic invertebrates — best treated as reef-safe with caution and watched around small ornamental inverts. Cross-source sizing spans roughly 2.5-3.5 inches (LiveAquaria 2.5 in, Reeflings 7 cm, FishBase 9 cm TL, Quality Marine ~4 in); 6-9 cm is the conservative envelope. Cross-source tank minimum spans 30 US gal (~114 L) to 100 L; bumped to 120 L because adults are territorial and benefit from extra footprint.
breedingNotes: Oviparous, with distinct pairing during breeding. Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate; the male guards and aerates them until hatching. Rarely targeted by aquarium breeders, and larval rearing has not been widely documented for the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level easy, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-compatible Yes, max 2.5 in, min 30 US gal, 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, omnivore. Catalog index page did not list the species so the direct product URL (found via search) was used in place of the slice's primary root.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacentridae), max length 9.0 cm TL, depth 0-32 m, Western Pacific distribution (Christmas Island to Fiji, southern Japan to Rowley Shoals/New Caledonia), preferred 25.5-29.3 C, oviparous with male egg-guarding, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Min 100 L (~26 US gal), 24-28 C, SG 1.023-1.026, pH 8-8.4, max 7 cm, reef-safe with monitoring, omnivore, semi-aggressive, male guards demersal eggs.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank (30 gal+), water parameters (72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025), max ~4 in, care level easy. Flagged as NOT reef-safe due to picking at benthic invertebrates — drove the 'with caution' reefSafe call.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs form during spawning; eggs are demersal and male-guarded. Rarely bred in the hobby; larval rearing has not been widely documented.
